title: Conditional — By, Bych, Kdyby
description: Learn the Czech conditional mood: polite requests with 'chtěl bych' (I would like), 'mohl bych' (I could), 'měl bych' (I should); the full conditional auxiliary conjugation (bych, bys, by, bychom, byste, by); 'kdybych' (if I...) clauses for hypotheticals; and 'přál bych si' (I would wish/I wish) for expressing desires.

data.explanation: 'Chtěl bych' = I would like (polite conditional). It's the most common polite way to order or request in Czech — much softer than 'Chci' (I want). The conditional uses the past participle 'chtěl' + auxiliary 'bych'. For female speaker: 'Chtěla bych kávu.'

exercises.prompt: What is the correct conditional auxiliary for 'ty' (you, informal)?

data.question_cz: Jaký je správný tvar podmiňovacího způsobu pro 'ty'?
data.question_en: What is the correct conditional auxiliary for 'ty' (you)?
data.explanation: The full conditional conjugation: já bych, ty bys, on/ona/ono by, my bychom, vy byste, oni by. 'Bys' is specifically for 'ty' (you singular informal). 'Byste' is for 'vy' (you plural or formal).

exercises.prompt: Complete the polite request: '___ bych kávu?' (Could I — using moci)

data.sentence: ___ bych kávu? (moci — could I have a coffee?)
data.explanation: 'Mohl bych kávu?' = Could I have a coffee? (male speaker). 'Mohla bych kávu?' (female speaker). The past participle of 'moci' is 'mohl' (masc) / 'mohla' (fem). 'Mohl bych' = I could. This is an extremely common polite phrase.

'Kdybych' = if I... (hypothetical/unreal condition). 'Kdybych měl čas' = If I had time (but I don't). 'Kdyby' conjugates like the conditional: kdybych, kdybys, kdyby, kdybychom, kdybyste, kdyby. It introduces unreal/hypothetical if-clauses. Compare with 'když' = when/if (real condition).

exercises.prompt: Translate to Czech: 'I would like a coffee.' (male speaker)

data.grammar_note: 'Chtěl bych' (masc) / 'Chtěla bych' (fem) = I would like. This is the standard polite form. Alternative: 'Dal bych si kávu.' = I would have a coffee (lit. 'I would give myself a coffee'). 'Kafe' is colloquial for káva.

exercises.prompt: Complete the conditional question: 'Would you (ty) like to go to the cinema?'

data.sentence: ___ bys jít do kina? (chtít — would you like)
data.explanation: 'Chtěl bys jít do kina?' (to a male) / 'Chtěla bys jít do kina?' (to a female). The past participle matches the subject's gender: 'chtěl' (masc), 'chtěla' (fem). 'Bys' is the auxiliary for 'ty'. 'Jít' = to go (on foot/by walking).

exercises.prompt: What is the correct translation of 'Přál bych si ...'?

data.explanation: 'Přál bych si' (masc) / 'Přála bych si' (fem) = I wish / I would wish. It's used to express desires and wishes: 'Přál bych si být bohatý.' = I wish I were rich. 'Přál bych si, abys přišel.' = I wish you would come. 'Přát si' = to wish (lit. 'to wish for oneself').

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'I should go home.' (mít — I should/I ought to)

data.sentence: ___ bych jít domů. (mít — I should go home, male)
data.explanation: 'Měl bych jít domů.' = I should go home (masc). 'Měla bych jít domů.' (fem). 'Měl bych / měla bych' + infinitive = I should / I ought to. This is the standard way to express obligation or advice softly. Compare: 'Musím jít domů.' = I must go home (strong obligation).

exercises.prompt: Translate to Czech: 'If I had money, I would buy a car.'

data.source: If I had money, I would buy a car.
accepted_answers: Kdybych měl peníze, koupil bych si auto.
Kdybych měla peníze, koupila bych si auto.
Kdybych měl peníze, koupil bych auto.
Kdybych měla peníze, koupila bych auto.
data.grammar_note: 'Kdybych měl/měla peníze' = if I had money (unreal — I don't have money). 'Koupil/koupila bych si auto' = I would buy a car. Both clauses use the conditional: 'kdybych' in the if-clause, 'bych' in the main clause. 'Peníze' = money (plural in Czech). 'Koupit si' = to buy for oneself.

exercises.answer_key: Kdybych měl peníze, koupil bych si auto.

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct conditional form for 'my' (we)?

data.question_cz: Jaký je správný tvar podmiňovacího způsobu pro 'my'?
data.question_en: Which is the correct conditional form for 'my' (we)?
data.explanation: 'Bychom' is the standard literary form for 'my' (we). In spoken Czech, 'bysme' is very common and widely accepted. Both are correct. Full forms: já bych, ty bys, on/ona/ono by, my bychom/bysme, vy byste, oni by.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'What would you (ty) do in my place?'

data.sentence: Co ___ dělal na mém místě? (ty — what would you do?)
data.explanation: 'Co bys dělal na mém místě?' (to a male) / 'Co bys dělala na mém místě?' (to a female) = What would you do in my place? 'Bys' is the conditional auxiliary for 'ty'. 'Na mém místě' = in my place (locative). This is a common hypothetical question pattern.

exercises.prompt: Complete the dialogue: ordering at a café using conditional.

lines.speaker: host
lines.text: ___ (I would have it with milk. / With milk, please.)

accepted_answers: Chtěl bych kávu, prosím|S mlékem, prosím
Chtěla bych kávu, prosím|S mlékem, prosím
Dal bych si kávu, prosím|S mlékem, prosím
Dala bych si kávu, prosím|S mlékem, prosím
data.explanation: 'Co si dáte?' = What will you have? (formal 'vy'). Polite response uses conditional: 'Chtěl/Chtěla bych...' or 'Dal/Dala bych si...'. 'S mlékem' = with milk, 'bez mléka' = without milk. 'Prosím' = please — always polite to add it when ordering.

exercises.answer_key: Chtěl bych kávu, prosím|S mlékem, prosím
